one_line_takeaway: Poisoning only the lightweight connector of a modular MLLM — with a single seed sample from one modality — implants a reusable latent-space backdoor that can be triggered from any other modality at inference (up to 99.9% ASR, 0% clean leakage), and existing model/input-side defenses cannot remove it without significant utility loss.
key_finding: The attack reaches up to 99.9% ASR in native-modality settings (>=99.5% across all backdoors on PandaGPT) and most cross-modal pairs exceed 95% ASR / CMR >0.950. The weakest cross-modal pair is image-activation under an audio door (CMR ~0.766, ASR 76.5%), consistent with that pair's largest initial modality gap. Stealth is strong: 0.0% backdoor leakage on clean inputs across all three doors, at most ~1.4% benign utility loss, and poisoned-vs-clean connector flattened weight cosine >=0.972 (0.998 for the image door). Against baselines under the image-door setting, Ours scores 99.5/99.2/99.4 (image/audio/text Exact ASR) versus PGD 81.5/98.5/99.2, UAP 0.0/0.0/97.0, BadNets 66.8, Blended 70.8, WaNet 56.2, SIG 63.4, and TrojVLM 39.0 (classical backdoors are image-only). Reachability: initial cosine to centroid 0.194–0.542 rises to >=0.952 (mostly >0.99) after PGD, with L2 distance dropping 86.7% on average. Defenses largely fail without heavy utility cost: connector fine-tuning still leaves >80% ASR after 20 epochs (image 81.0%); fine-pruning keeps >96% ASR at 30% pruning and only collapses at 40% pruning (image ASR 4.0%) at an ~11% (0.020 SPICE) utility drop; JPEG/Gaussian blur zero out non-adaptive ASR but an adaptive adversary recovers 82.5–93.0% (JPEG), 97.0–98.0% (blur), and ~99% (bit-depth).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is an "asymmetric cross-modal backdoor" attack against connector-based multimodal LLMs (MLLMs). The key novelty over prior backdoor work (which the authors explicitly frame as single-modality/symmetric, e.g. BadEncoder, BadCLIP, Shadowcast, TrojVLM in Related Work) is that the adversary poisons ONLY the lightweight connector using a single seed sample plus augmented variants from ONE modality, yet the backdoor becomes activatable from OTHER, never-poisoned modalities at inference. The mechanism they contribute is a three-phase pipeline: (1) connector poisoning that carves a compact malicious region into the shared latent space while preserving benign alignment; (2) extraction of a "malicious centroid" (decoupling direction and magnitude) as a stable latent anchor; (3) input-side PGD optimization at inference to steer clean inputs from any modality toward that centroid, requiring no repeated API queries and no full-model access. They position the shared latent alignment itself as the trigger carrier, and the connector as a high-leverage supply-chain attack surface.
threat_model: Supply-chain scenario for open-source MLLM assembly. The adversary is a malicious third-party connector provider who compromises ONLY the lightweight, plug-and-play connector before release (local white-box control over connector fine-tuning) while the victim independently selects and freezes trusted encoders and LLM backbones. The adversary uses limited poisoning data from a single publicly-available modality (no access to the victim's private data, logs, queries, or benchmarks), and at inference performs zero-query activation: submitting pre-crafted bounded-perturbation inputs through the victim's standard API without parameter access or repeated queries. Attack goals are preserving clean utility, avoiding unconditional leakage, and surviving post-deployment defenses.
limitations: Author-acknowledged: the text-modality attack is an embedding-space diagnostic (continuous perturbations injected into ImageBind embeddings), not a valid discrete-token text attack, since natural-language tokens cannot support the differentiable optimization required for reconstruction. Scope is limited to the MLP-projector connector paradigm (Q-Former and cross-attention connectors are noted but not attacked). Cross-modal success degrades when the modality gap is large under a fixed budget (image-under-audio CMR ~0.766). Evaluation covers only two target architectures (PandaGPT, NExT-GPT), both ImageBind/shared-latent-space style any-to-any systems, so generality to other MLLM families is asserted but not broadly demonstrated. Encoder/LLM fine-tuning could weaken the attack but is dismissed as impractical rather than empirically tested at scale, and no real deployed service was attacked (isolated local, public models/datasets, harmless target phrase only).

datasets_used: MS COCO (5,000 images, 25,000 captions; image and text inputs)
quantitative_results: Native-modality ASR up to 99.9%; >=99.5% across all backdoors on PandaGPT
Most cross-modal pairs exceed 95% ASR and CMR >0.950; weakest is image-under-audio door CMR ~0.766 (ASR 76.5%)
Backdoor leakage 0.0% on clean inputs across all three doors
Benign utility degradation at most ~1.4%; image-door metric shifts within 0.0010 (BLEU-4/ROUGE-L increase for audio/text doors)
Poisoned-vs-clean connector flattened weight cosine 0.998 (image), >=0.972 (audio/text); relative Frobenius change 0.064/0.238/0.214
Image-door baseline Exact/Relaxed ASR: Ours 99.5/99.2/99.4 vs PGD 81.5/98.5/99.2, UAP 0.0/0.0/97.0, BadNets 66.8, Blended 70.8, WaNet 56.2, SIG 63.4, TrojVLM 39.0 (image/audio/text)
Reachability: initial cosine 0.194–0.542 -> final >=0.952 (mostly >0.99); L2 distance reduced 86.7% on average
Poisoning uses 1 anchor + 49 augmented variants + 450 clean samples (poisoning rate 0.1); PGD <=500 steps; image budget <=32/255, audio/text <=0.1

Model-side defense: connector fine-tuning leaves >80% ASR after 20 epochs (image 81.0%); fine-pruning keeps >96% ASR at 30% pruning, collapses only at 40% (image 4.0%, audio 15.0%, text 25.0%) at ~0.020 SPICE (~11%) utility drop

Input-side defense: JPEG and Gaussian blur (sigma>=1.0) drop non-adaptive ASR to 0.0%, but adaptive attack recovers 82.5–93.0% (JPEG), 97.0–98.0% (blur), ~99% (bit-depth); bit-depth 6-bit leaves ASR 98.9% non-adaptively
